sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
A biomedical device team designs a capacitive pressure sensor for a catheter tip. The sensor contains two circular conducting plates separated by a flexible dielectric membrane. When external pressure increases, the membrane compresses slightly, reducing plate separation. The circuit measures capacitance and converts it to pressure using C = κϵ₀A/d.

Condition | Plate area | Separation d | Dielectric κ | Measured capacitance
Baseline | A | 20 μm | 2.0 | 8.9 pF
Compressed | A | 10 μm | 2.0 | 17.8 pF
Membrane swap | A | 20 μm | 4.0 | 17.8 pF

In a calibration test, the charged sensor is disconnected from the battery before compression. The team notes that charge on the isolated plates remains approximately constant, while voltage can change. They also test whether stray ions in saline create a resistive leakage path. In this test, the baseline sensor is charged, disconnected from the battery, and placed in saline while voltage is recorded over time. The decay is modeled as V(t) = V₀e^(−t/RC) using the baseline capacitance; the resulting trace is shown in Figure 1.
questions.stem: Why does capacitance double when the membrane is compressed from 20 μm to 10 μm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capacitance is directly proportional to plate separation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capacitance is inversely proportional to plate separation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capacitance is independent of plate separation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capacitance decreases because the electric field is eliminated.

explanations.A: Direct proportionality would predict that halving d halves C, but the table shows C doubles. This reverses the capacitor equation. In a parallel-plate capacitor, bringing plates closer increases the ability to store charge at a given voltage because opposite charges interact more strongly across the smaller gap.
explanations.B: Correct. For a parallel-plate capacitor, C = κϵ₀A/d. Area and dielectric constant are unchanged between baseline and compression, so halving d from 20 μm to 10 μm doubles C. The table's 8.9 pF to 17.8 pF change is a direct numerical check of the inverse relationship.
explanations.C: If C were independent of d, the compressed condition would remain 8.9 pF. The passage intentionally varies separation while holding A and κ constant, isolating d as the causal variable. Ignoring d would also make the pressure-sensing mechanism impossible.
explanations.D: Compression does not eliminate the electric field; it changes geometry and therefore capacitance. A capacitor can maintain an electric field between plates when charged. The measured increase in capacitance contradicts the idea that the field has disappeared.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Leakage discharge of an isolated capacitive sensor submerged in saline. Dashed lines mark the time constant τ and corresponding voltage V₀/e.

questions.stem: The membrane swap produces the same capacitance as compression because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: doubling κ offsets the original plate separation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: dielectrics remove all stored charge from the plates.

choices.label: C
choices.text: larger κ always increases plate separation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: capacitance depends only on voltage.

explanations.A: Correct. The membrane swap doubles κ from 2.0 to 4.0 while keeping d at 20 μm, and C is directly proportional to κ. Compression doubles C by halving d; the membrane swap doubles C by doubling dielectric constant. Different physical changes can therefore produce the same capacitance.
explanations.B: A dielectric polarizes in the electric field and reduces the effective field for a given free charge; it does not remove all charge from conducting plates. If charge vanished, the sensor would not store energy or produce the measured capacitance signal.
explanations.C: The dielectric constant describes polarization response, not a mechanical command to increase separation. The table states that separation remains 20 μm during the membrane swap. The equal capacitance comes from κ, not hidden motion of the plates.
explanations.D: Capacitance is a geometric/material property of the capacitor, not merely a function of whatever voltage is applied. Voltage, charge, and capacitance are related by Q = CV, but the table manipulates κ and d to change C itself.

questions.stem: If the charged sensor is disconnected from the battery before compression, what happens to voltage across the plates as capacitance increases?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Voltage increases because Q remains constant and C increases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Voltage remains fixed because the battery still controls it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Voltage decreases because Q remains constant and V = Q/C.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Voltage becomes zero because capacitance doubled.

explanations.A: This reverses Q = CV. For an isolated capacitor, free charge is approximately fixed, so increasing capacitance lowers the voltage needed to hold that charge. Voltage would increase only if capacitance fell at constant charge.
explanations.B: The passage explicitly says the sensor is disconnected from the battery before compression. Without a battery, voltage is not clamped by an external source. Confusing isolated and battery-connected capacitors is the central trap in this question.
explanations.C: Correct. Once disconnected, the capacitor is isolated, so Q remains roughly constant. Since V = Q/C, doubling capacitance cuts voltage in half. This differs from a battery-connected capacitor, where V is fixed and extra charge flows to match the new capacitance.
explanations.D: Doubling capacitance does not make voltage zero unless capacitance became infinite or charge were removed. The plates still hold charge, so a finite voltage remains. The change is proportional, not an on/off collapse.

questions.stem: Which change would most directly increase capacitance without changing membrane material or separation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decrease plate area.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase leakage current through saline.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Disconnect the voltmeter.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increase plate area.

explanations.A: Decreasing plate area would reduce capacitance because C is proportional to A. Smaller overlap gives the plates less capacity to store charge at a given voltage. This option changes the right variable but in the wrong direction.
explanations.B: Leakage current discharges the capacitor over time but does not directly increase its geometric capacitance. It would create measurement drift or loss of stored charge, not a larger C in the parallel-plate equation.
explanations.C: Disconnecting a voltmeter changes the measurement circuit, not the capacitor's physical capacitance. Unless the meter itself loads the circuit, removing it does not alter A, d, or κ. The best answer must change a variable in the capacitance equation.
explanations.D: Correct. With κ and d fixed, C = κϵ₀A/d shows that capacitance increases linearly with plate area. Larger plates provide more surface for charge separation, allowing more charge storage at the same voltage.

questions.stem: Based on Figure 1 and the baseline capacitance, the leakage resistance is closest to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 × 10¹¹ Ω

explanations.A: Correct. For an RC discharge, the time constant τ is the time required for voltage to fall to about 37% of its initial value. Figure 1 shows V ≈ 0.37V₀ at about 2 s. Using the baseline capacitance, C = 8.9 pF = 8.9 × 10⁻¹² F, R = τ/C ≈ 2.0/(8.9 × 10⁻¹²) ≈ 2.2 × 10¹¹ Ω. The large resistance reflects a very small leakage current pathway through saline.
explanations.B: This value is too small by roughly three orders of magnitude. It would result from treating picofarads as nanofarads or otherwise using a capacitance about 1000 times too large. The passage reports C in pF, so the conversion to farads is essential.
explanations.C: This has the units and magnitude of a capacitance-like quantity, not a resistance. Resistance is found by dividing time by capacitance in τ = RC; multiplying or inverting the wrong quantity gives an unphysical answer.
explanations.D: This also confuses the capacitance scale with resistance. A resistance on the order of 10⁻¹² Ω would discharge essentially instantly, not over seconds. The seconds-long voltage decay in Figure 1 requires a very large leakage resistance.

questions.passage:
A second group studies electric fields near charged drug-delivery nanoparticles. Each particle is approximated as a point charge in dilute solution before proteins adsorb to its surface. The electric potential at distance r from a point charge is modeled as V = kq/r, and the electric field magnitude is E = k|q|/r². A positively charged particle has q = +2.0 nC.

Distance from center | Potential V | Field magnitude E
1.0 mm | V₁ | E₁
2.0 mm | V₂ | E₂
4.0 mm | V₄ | E₄

When serum proteins bind, the effective surface charge decreases because oppositely charged residues partially screen the particle. The researchers emphasize that potential is a scalar, electric field is a vector, and field direction for a positive charge points radially outward. In a later assay, two positive particles repel each other in solution.
questions.stem: How does electric potential change when distance from the positive particle doubles from 1.0 mm to 2.0 mm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It doubles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It becomes half as large.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It becomes one fourth as large.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It changes sign.

explanations.A: Doubling would require direct proportionality to r, but point-charge potential follows V = kq/r. Moving farther away reduces potential magnitude. This option confuses distance dependence with a variable like circumference or path length.
explanations.B: Correct. For a point charge, V = kq/r, so doubling r halves V. Because q is positive, the potential remains positive; only its magnitude decreases. The result differs from the electric field, which would decrease by a factor of four.
explanations.C: One fourth is the inverse-square pattern for electric field magnitude, not electric potential. The passage includes both equations to test that distinction. Potential falls as 1/r, while field magnitude falls as 1/r².
explanations.D: Changing distance does not change the sign of the source charge. The potential from a positive charge remains positive at all listed distances. Sign would change if q changed from positive to negative, not if r doubled.

questions.stem: How does electric field magnitude change when distance doubles from 1.0 mm to 2.0 mm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It becomes half as large.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It doubles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It becomes one fourth as large.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It remains unchanged.

explanations.A: Half as large describes potential, not field. Electric field magnitude depends on 1/r², so distance has a stronger effect. The distractor is tempting because the previous relationship V = kq/r is nearby in the passage.
explanations.B: The field does not double when distance increases; it decreases. A larger separation weakens the force per unit test charge. This option reverses the physical intuition that fields are strongest near their source charges.
explanations.C: Correct. Electric field magnitude for a point charge is E = k|q|/r². Doubling r multiplies the denominator by 4, so E becomes one fourth of its original value. This inverse-square dependence also underlies Coulomb force between point charges.
explanations.D: A constant field would be a reasonable approximation between large parallel plates, but not around a point charge. The passage explicitly models the nanoparticle as point-like in dilute solution, so E must vary with r.

questions.stem: What is the direction of the electric field created by the positively charged particle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Radially inward toward the particle.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clockwise around the particle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Parallel to the particle's velocity only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Radially outward from the particle.

explanations.A: Radially inward is the field direction around a negative point charge, because a positive test charge would be attracted inward. The particle in the passage is positive, so a positive test charge would be repelled outward.
explanations.B: Circular field lines around a source are associated with magnetic fields around currents, not electrostatic fields from a stationary point charge. Electrostatic field lines begin on positive charge and end on negative charge or infinity.
explanations.C: The electrostatic field from a point charge is determined by charge and position, not by the particle's velocity in this model. Velocity would become relevant for magnetic effects, which are not the field described by E = k|q|/r².
explanations.D: Correct. Electric field direction is defined as the direction of force on a positive test charge. A positive nanoparticle repels a positive test charge, so its field points radially outward. Potential is scalar, but field has this vector direction.

questions.stem: What happens to potential and field magnitude if adsorbed proteins reduce the effective charge magnitude by half at the same distance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potential halves and field magnitude halves.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potential halves but field magnitude quarters.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potential quarters but field magnitude halves.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both become negative.

explanations.A: Correct. At fixed r, both V = kq/r and E = k|q|/r² are directly proportional to charge magnitude. Halving effective charge halves both potential and field magnitude. The different distance exponents do not matter when distance is held constant.
explanations.B: This incorrectly applies inverse-square reasoning to charge rather than distance. Field is inverse-square with r, but it is linear in |q|. Protein screening that halves effective charge therefore halves E, not quarters it.
explanations.C: Potential is linear in q, so it does not quarter when charge halves. This option mixes up charge scaling and distance scaling. The passage's equations show that both quantities depend linearly on the source charge.
explanations.D: Screening reduces magnitude but does not necessarily reverse sign. The proteins partially reduce positive effective charge; they do not make the particle net negative in the described scenario. Potential remains positive unless the effective charge changes sign.

questions.stem: Why do two positive particles repel each other?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coulomb force is always attractive regardless of charge sign.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive charges cannot create electric fields.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Like charges exert forces away from each other along the line connecting them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force is perpendicular to the electric field.

explanations.A: Coulomb force can be attractive or repulsive depending on the signs of the charges. Opposite charges attract; like charges repel. This option erases the sign dependence that the passage highlights with two positive particles.
explanations.B: Positive charges do create electric fields; their field lines point outward. If they could not create fields, the potential and field measurements in the passage would be meaningless. The observed repulsion depends precisely on those fields.
explanations.C: Correct. Coulomb's law predicts that charges with the same sign repel, with force directed along the line connecting them. Each positive particle sits in the outward electric field of the other and experiences a force away from the other particle.
explanations.D: The electric force on a charge is F = qE, so for a positive charge it points in the same direction as the electric field. A perpendicular force would be characteristic of magnetic force on a moving charge, not electrostatic repulsion.
